senses.definition: a small bony or cartilaginous support structure that bears fin rays in fishes and relates to muscle attachments near the fin base
examples: The pterygophore supports the dorsal fin rays in many teleost fishes.
Fossil specimens show the arrangement of pterygophores along the vertebral column.

senses.definition: an anatomical element that functions as a fin-supporting structure in sharks or related fishes, typically binding fin rays or attaching musculature
examples: Pterygopodium elements anchor the fin to the skeleton in certain elasmobranchs.
Researchers described the pterygopodium as part of the fin-support system.

senses.definition: of, relating to, or forming the upper half of the first pharyngeal arch in lower vertebrates, contributing to upper jaw formation
examples: The pterygoquadrate component is involved in the development of the upper jaw.
In some amphibians, the pterygoquadrate region is fused to other skull bones.

senses.definition: a bone in the skull area connecting the sphenoid region to pterygoid elements, used in descriptions of reptile and vertebrate skull anatomy
examples: The pterygosphenoid structure helps define the skull base in some reptiles.
Anatomical diagrams label the pterygosphenoid near the sphenoid complex.

senses.definition: relating to a wing-shaped spine or a wing-like bony projection, especially in skull or pterygoid region
examples: A pterygospinous bar may be present in certain skull anatomies.
Radiographs sometimes show a pterygospinous process.

senses.definition: a small bone or projection in the pterygoid-sphenoid area of skull anatomy, often referenced in detailed vertebrate skull descriptions
examples: The pterygostaphyline is noted in some comparative anatomy diagrams.
Researchers described the pterygostaphyline as part of the palatoquadrate complex.

senses.definition: a large subclass of winged or capable-of-flight insects that includes most living insects; used in biology to refer to winged insects as a group
examples: Pterygota includes most familiar insects such as butterflies and beetles.
The winged forms are common in the order Diptera within Pterygota.

senses.definition: having wings or capable of flight; used especially of insects that develop wings at some stage
examples: Most pterygote insects have wings at some life stage.
The fossil shows a pterygote form with fully developed wings.

senses.definition: having a wing-like projection or development; used primarily in anatomical descriptions
examples: The pterygotous process contributes to the wing-like expansion of the skull bones.
A pterygotous feature is observed in some fossil specimens.

senses.definition: relating to wing-like structures associated with trabeculae or to a rib-like framework in anatomy
examples: The pterygotrabecular region describes a rib-like structural area.
Paleontologists note pterygotrabecular features in the skull base.

senses.definition: an extinct genus of primitive sea scorpions (eurypterids), known from fossil records
examples: Pterygotus is a genus of large ancient eurypterids.
Fossils of Pterygotus provide insight into Silurian aquatic life.

senses.definition: one of the tracts on a bird’s skin where feathers develop; a feather tract
examples: Feathers form along the pterylae of the bird’s skin.
Different pterylae patterns help identify species.

senses.definition: the collective terms for the feather tracts on a bird’s skin
examples: Pterylae determine where feathers can form.
The atlas shows the arrangement of pterylae across the bird’s body.

senses.definition: relating to the description or writing about feather tracts (pterylography)
examples: The study offers a pterylographic overview of avian skin.
Pterylographic data helps classify bird species.

senses.definition: relating to pterylography or the study of feather tracts
examples: The pterylographical record records feather tract patterns.
Pterylographical methods reveal growth zones in feathers.

senses.definition: collections or studies of pterylography; writings about feather tracts
examples: Several pterylographies were published to document feather tract patterns.
Her pterylographies examined evolutionary changes in plumage.

senses.definition: the study or description of feather tracts on birds (pterylography)
examples: Pterylography is used to understand feather placement in birds.
The book includes methods of pterylography for taxonomy.

senses.definition: relating to ptyerylology, the study of feather tracts in birds
examples: Pterylological diagrams map feather tracts precisely.
A pterylological atlas assists in identifying bird species.

senses.definition: the science or study of feather tracts (pterylogy)
examples: Pterylology helps classify birds by feather patterns.
Historical pterylology influenced early avian taxonomy.

senses.definition: plural of pterylosis; conditions involving feather tract development or loss in birds
examples: Pteryloses refer to feather tract anomalies observed in some species.
Researchers documented several pteryloses in the flight apparatus.

senses.definition: a condition affecting feather tract development in birds; abnormal feather tract formation
examples: Pterylosis can alter the feather pattern on a bird.
Anatomical texts describe pterylosis in various species.

senses.definition: a taxonomic term related to wing-bearing or feather-related structures in certain vertebrates; used in comparative anatomy
examples: Pteryrygia describes certain wing-associated skull features in fossil records.
The term appears in advanced anatomical texts.

senses.definition: an abbreviation with context-dependent meaning; not itself a standalone dictionary sense without the expansion
examples: PTG may be used as an abbreviation in technical notes; expand accordingly.
In this document, PTG stands for a specific project group.

senses.definition: a family name for an extinct group of prehistoric fish; used in paleontology
examples: Pt-ichthyidae is cited in fossil classifications.
Paleontologists described species within the Ptilichthyidae family.

senses.definition: a taxonomic family name in paleontology or ichthyology; used for certain extinct or primitive fish
examples: Ptiliidae is a family described in early vertebrate studies.
Fossils attributed to Ptiliidae show primitive jaw structures.
